trending_skills.name: Chrome Gemini Patch
trending_skills.description: CVE-2026-0628 allowed malicious Chrome extensions to access camera and microphone through Gemini's AI panel — no extra permissions needed. Google patched it, but you need to update Chrome. If your agent operates browser automation, verify you're on latest Chrome.
trending_skills.install: Update Chrome to latest version. No skill needed — this is a PSA.
trending_skills.why_trending: Critical vulnerability. Silent surveillance through a trusted UI element. Everyone running browser-connected agents should verify their Chrome version.

trending_skills.name: OpenClaw Data Analysis
trending_skills.description: New Medium deep-dive on using OpenClaw's skill system for data analysis and visualization workflows. Covers the five-component architecture and ReAct reasoning loop applied to data tasks.
trending_skills.why_trending: Best technical breakdown of OpenClaw's architecture for data teams. If you want your agent to analyze CSVs, query databases, or build charts — this is the playbook.

trending_skills.name: 10 Practical OpenClaw Workflows
trending_skills.description: From inbox automation to agent bots — a comprehensive guide covering email triage, calendar management, file organization, code review, research, and social monitoring. All with real examples.
trending_skills.why_trending: Published yesterday. Fills the gap between 'I installed OpenClaw' and 'my agent actually does useful things.' Good for sharing with new users.

ai_tweets.headline: Agent framework wars heat up: OpenClaw vs CrewAI vs AutoGPT vs LangGraph
ai_tweets.detail: Three separate comparison articles published this week. Consensus: OpenClaw = best personal agent (local-first, skill ecosystem), CrewAI = best team simulation (roles + handoffs), LangGraph = best for complex workflows (graph-based), AutoGPT = best sandboxing.
ai_tweets.takeaway: OpenClaw wins on personal automation and skill ecosystem. CrewAI wins on multi-agent team simulation. Know when to use which.

ai_tweets.headline: AWS Strands + OpenAI partnership: Amazon enters the agent framework game
ai_tweets.detail: Amazon launched Strands Labs — a multi-agent orchestration framework — alongside their OpenAI partnership. Cloud-native approach vs OpenClaw's local-first philosophy.
ai_tweets.takeaway: Big tech is validating the agent framework category. OpenClaw's advantage: open source, local-first, community skills. AWS's advantage: enterprise integration + scale.

ai_tweets.headline: CVE-2026-2256: From AI prompt to full system compromise
ai_tweets.detail: New CVE demonstrates how crafted content injected into agent data sources (prompts, documents, logs) can achieve full system compromise without direct shell access. Affects any agent that processes untrusted inputs.
ai_tweets.takeaway: If your agent processes external documents, emails, or web content, it's vulnerable. Sandboxing (NanoClaw containers) and input validation are no longer optional.

prompt_hack.title: The 'Agent Decision Log' — Never Lose Context Again
steps: Add to AGENTS.md: 'After every significant decision or completed task, append a one-line entry to memory/decisions-YYYY-MM.md with format: [date] [decision] [reasoning] [outcome]'
Your agent now maintains a searchable log of every decision it made and why. Next session, it can reference past reasoning instead of starting from scratch.
prompt_hack.why_it_matters: Agents wake up fresh every session. Without explicit decision logging, they'll re-ask questions you already answered or reverse decisions they already made. This simple pattern gives your agent institutional memory.

model_radar.detail: Oflight published a detailed comparison: OpenClaw = fully local (only API calls external), Claude Code = cloud, ChatGPT = cloud, AutoGPT = local with API calls, CrewAI = local with API calls. OpenClaw is the only framework where processing stays entirely on your machine.
model_radar.relevance: Privacy-conscious users: OpenClaw is confirmed as the most local-first option. Your prompts, files, and context never leave your machine (only API calls to your chosen model provider).

model_radar.detail: New entrant comparing itself to CrewAI, LangGraph, and AutoGen. Focuses on inter-framework agent communication. Early stage but interesting architecture.
model_radar.relevance: Worth watching if you need agents from different frameworks to talk to each other. Not production-ready yet.

community_build.title: 24/7 Personal Agent — Lessons from 30 Days of Always-On Operation
community_build.description: Every.to published a deep dive on running a personal agent 24/7 for a full month. Key lessons: heartbeat-driven proactive checks work, memory files are essential for continuity, skill composition beats monolithic prompts, and the biggest win was email triage saving 45 minutes per day.
community_build.how_to_replicate: Start with: heartbeat every 30 min, 3 checked items (email, calendar, files). Add memory/daily files for context. Expand slowly — don't automate everything on day 1.
